video_title: Ukraine's Mid-Range Strike Campaign - The War Against Russian Supply Lines & Air Defence
gist: Perun analyzes Ukraine's 2026 ramp-up of 'mid-range strike' (roughly 30-300km behind the front), a drone-driven interdiction campaign against Russian trucks, trains and depots that fills the gap between tactical FPV strikes and long-range strategic attacks. He details the enabling tech (fixed-wing loitering munitions like the Hornet, repurposed FP2 heavy drones, Starlink and autonomous targeting), a southern case study where Ukraine has sequentially closed Russia's sea, ferry and bridge supply routes to Crimea, and argues the effect is not running Russia out of trucks but forcing costly defensive resource diversion and civilian-logistics strain.

themes.theme: The mid-range strike concept and its strategic logic

insights.claim: Ukraine has opened a distinct 'mid-range' strike layer targeting roughly 30-50km out to 200-300km behind the front, hitting depots, supply lines, trucks and trains rather than frontline forces or deep strategic targets.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Defines a new operational-depth interdiction tier that extends the drone threat envelope and reshapes how militaries must protect rear logistics 100-200km from the front.
insights.quote: a battle not so much of trenches and tree lines as trucks and trains

insights.claim: The campaign's effect is best measured by non-events: Russian attacks that don't happen, Ukrainian territory not taken, and force-generation that falls short because manpower and air defense are diverted to rear protection.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Reframes how to assess interdiction effectiveness; the payoff shows up as suppressed Russian offensive capacity rather than destroyed-truck counts.
insights.quote: some of the biggest signs of a successful campaign here might actually be non-events

insights.claim: You don't need to destroy a transportation system to make it notably less efficient: pushing depots from ~50km to ~100km out of HIMARS range cut effective truck throughput by ~33% while increasing vehicle and driver hours.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Quantifies how interdiction degrades logistics efficiency without total destruction; a transferable model for evaluating supply-line attrition.
insights.quote: you don't need the capacity to destroy a transportation system in order to make it notably less efficient

insights.claim: Russia's artillery fire rate has never recovered to its pre-HIMARS level even ~4 years later, partly because GMLRS forced ammunition depots to disperse and move further back.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Evidence that precision rear-area interdiction produces durable, not transient, reductions in enemy combat output.
insights.quote: we have never seen Russia's artillery fire rate recover to what it was before the arrival of the Gimla's rocket

insights.claim: Lightweight fixed-wing loitering munitions like the Hornet (~15kg takeoff, 2.2m wingspan, ~5kg warhead, ~130-160km range, pneumatic catapult launch) act as cheap 'truck hunters' with good autonomous target recognition, low-light cameras and quiet operation.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: These cheap fixed-wing munitions make it economical to hit individual soft-skin vehicles deep behind the line, a capability HIMARS lacked.
insights.quote: Russian sources reportedly estimate the range of the thing at about 130 to 145 km

insights.claim: Autonomous target recognition lets a drone lock a target while still in communication range, so even if electronic warfare severs the link during the terminal dive, the strike often still completes, a key EW-resistance advantage at mid-range distances.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Autonomous targeting plus Starlink comms is the technical breakthrough enabling reliable deep-strike against moving targets where fiber/RF links fail.
insights.quote: the targeting decision has already made while the system was out of EW range

insights.claim: Ukraine's FP2 trades the FP1's ~1,400km range down to ~200km to carry a 121kg bomb with ~44kg explosive filler (with plans for ~200kg warheads), optimizing heavy one-way drones for static mid-range targets like storage buildings and air-defense radars.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Shows a deliberate two-tier munitions strategy: cheap Hornets for trucks, heavy FP2s for high-value fixed targets, rather than a single platform.
insights.quote: there's about three times as much explosive filler in an FP2 than there is total mass in a Hornet

insights.claim: Range-extension experiments (balloon-hoisting a Hornet to altitude) could push ranges toward ~300km, and rocket-firing reusable drones plus FPV mother-ships point to standoff and reusable loitering-munition concepts still in early development.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Signals the threat envelope and cost-efficiency will keep improving; reusable standoff drones could blunt Russian hard-kill defenses.

themes.theme: Southern case study: closing Russia's Crimea supply routes

insights.claim: The south is uniquely vulnerable because beyond Zaporizhzhia, Kherson and Crimea there is only water, so Russian supply lines are strung out and cannot shorten by retreating, unlike fronts that move closer to Russia proper.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Geography makes southern interdiction disproportionately impactful, the highest-leverage sector for Ukrainian supply-line attacks.
insights.quote: beyond Zaparisia, Herasan and Crimea, you don't have more Russia. You have water

insights.claim: Ukraine has sequentially pinched off Russia's Crimea supply options: naval cargo shipping (storm shadow + naval drones made it too risky), rail ferries (all three main ferries Slavyan, Avangard, Conro Trader hit, with 5-day waits), and the Crimean Bridge (self-restricted by Russia to non-explosive cargo after the sabotage attack).
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Each closed route funnels Russian fuel/ammo onto the one remaining northern rail route through occupied Ukraine, exactly within mid-range drone reach.
insights.quote: there were three railway fairies, all of which are screwed

insights.claim: Geolocated mid-range strikes on Russian logistics 30-150km behind the front jumped from 2 in Feb 2026 to 15 in March, 55 in April and 130 in May, while Ukrainian MoD soft-skin vehicle claims roughly doubled year-over-year to ~8,000 in May.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Documents a steep, accelerating ramp that, if it continues, could overwhelm Russian rear defenses in key sectors.
insights.quote: In February 2026, they recorded a grand total of two visually confirmed strikes... In May, 130

insights.claim: The loss-ratio of Russian trucks to armored vehicles flipped from roughly even in Q4 2025 to over 3:1 trucks per armored vehicle in Q2 2026, driven both by fewer armored targets near the front and more truck strikes deeper in the rear.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Marks a structural shift in Ukrainian targeting from frontline armor to rear logistics, the signature of the mid-range campaign.
insights.quote: in quarter 2, it was more than three and a bit trucks for every bit of armor

themes.theme: Why interdiction matters despite Russia's deep truck reserves

insights.claim: Ukraine cannot run Russia out of trucks: Russia orders ~3,800 trucks/year from KamAZ, ~1,200 from Ural, produces ~7,600 vans/year, can press millions of civilian vehicles into service, and could import a fleet from China, so the replacement pool is effectively infinite.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Tempers 'Russia will run out of trucks' headlines; the strategic value is efficiency degradation and resource diversion, not fleet destruction.
insights.quote: the world and Russia just has so many of them

insights.claim: The decisive lever is civilian logistics: civilian transport firms (which move much of Crimea's supply for ~2.5M residents) have lower risk tolerance than the military, already demanding bonuses or refusing routes, and Russian bans on civilian highway traffic effectively flag every truck as a valid drone target.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Interdiction works by scaring off civilian capacity, forcing the military to absorb more logistics burden at lower efficiency, the real second-order effect.
insights.quote: civilian operators tend to have a very different perception of acceptable risk than their military counterparts

insights.claim: Crimea is already rationing fuel: authorities temporarily banned petrol sales to non-military/emergency vehicles, then resumed under tightened rationing, an early concrete sign of supply strain on the civilian economy.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Civilian fuel rationing is a leading indicator of system stress, suggesting interdiction effects are already biting beyond claims data.
insights.quote: Russian authorities in Crimea placed a temporary ban on the sale of petrol to anything other than military or emergency vehicles

insights.claim: Russia's main defensive counter, hundreds of mobile fire groups and observation posts along key roads, would require several thousand additional personnel and hundreds of vehicles, and remains vulnerable to Ukraine targeting the posts, massing strikes on convoys, or simply shifting drones to softer targets elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: The defensive solution is manpower- and resource-intensive and easily countered, reinforcing the campaign's value as a Russian resource sink.
insights.quote: We'd need several thousand people, hundreds of pieces of equipment and machinery

insights.claim: Mid-strike synergizes with Ukraine's long-range strike and air-defense-suppression efforts by stretching Russian air defenses thin across refineries, Moscow, St. Petersburg and supply lines, making each individual campaign more likely to succeed.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: The campaigns are mutually reinforcing; Russian air-defense overstretch is a compounding vulnerability across all strike tiers.
insights.quote: the massive synergy with both Ukraine's we have seat and DAD at home effort and the longrange strike effort

insights.claim: The broader lesson for global militaries is that the drone kill zone keeps expanding rearward, so forces used to feeling secure 100-200km behind the front, even with air superiority, should expect that sanctuary to erode.
insights.speaker: Perun
insights.so_what: Signals a doctrinal shift with implications for defense planning and rear-area protection investment worldwide.
insights.quote: the threat range and the lethality of the drone kill zone continues to expand further and further back
